kanji.meaning_mnemonic: There is an <radical>insect</radical> on the <radical>roof</radical> in a <radical>spoon</radical>. It's there for the eating, and it's how you plan to attract a <kanji>snake</kanji> to your roof.
On the <radical>insect</radical> <radical>signpost</radical> there is a single sign that says <kanji>bee</kanji>.
If you <radical>receive</radical> something edible, arrange it in a <radical>circle</radical> and <radical>boil</radical> it. That way, it’ll <kanji>ripen</kanji> more quickly.

Pointing a <radical>thread</radical> to the <radical>east</radical> is what you <kanji>practice</kanji> every day. You're incredibly passionate about eastward thread-pointing, so you make sure to practice every day.
A <radical>yurt</radical>'s <radical>stool</radical> is always placed at certain <kanji>degree</kanji>s. The degrees change depending on specific <kanji>occurrence</kanji>s.

If someone has their <radical>fingers</radical> in your <radical>syrup</radical>, it's just another way of saying they <kanji>manipulate</kanji>d you. Cause only slimy people manipulate other people, so their fingers are slimy and sticky because they're covered in your syrup from mucking up your life.

This also has another meaning: <kanji>chastity</kanji>. Just imagine if someone wanted you to keep your chastity and they called s.e.x. when someone "puts their fingers in your syrup." That would be pretty effective, right? Cause it's like, super gross.
The <radical>thread</radical> radical and <kanji>thread</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.

This kanji is made up of the ground radical (which also happens to be the kanji for "one") and the radical for two. Combine <radical>one</radical> and <radical>two</radical> together and what do you get? <kanji>Three</kanji>!
In the <radical>cage</radical> there are some <radical>legs</radical>. They are the legs attached to a <kanji>small animal</kanji>.
Remember how the radical version of this kanji meant <radical>person</radical> because it looks like a person with their arms down and their legs spread out? Well this is a <kanji>person</kanji> too!

If someone is headed in your <radical>direction</radical> with a <radical>gun</radical>, nock an <radical>arrow</radical> in your bow and gather your <kanji>tribe</kanji> behind you because it’s about to get real! This man with a gun is headed your direction and he doesn’t even know what you and your tribe are gonna do to him. If he gets any closer, you’re likely to shoot that arrow right at him.

If you want to <radical>dry</radical> your <radical>horns</radical> you need to lay them <kanji>flat</kanji>. If they aren't flat they'll always be a little damp somewhere and you'll never have a moment's <kanji>peace</kanji>.

You have some <radical>dirt</radical> next to a <radical>pool</radical> that has an extra <radical>drop</radical> of water in it because you need them to be <kanji>equal</kanji>. If there's too much dirt outside the pool and not enough water inside, it's gonna look weird. We can't have that.

See that <radical>gold</radical> man <radical>oversee</radical>ing everyone? He's a <kanji>model</kanji> human. His goldenness is a sign of how exemplary he is, and he's overseeing all of us flawed, regular folks to try to help us get on his level.
You build a <radical>roof</radical> over your <radical>coat rack</radical> because you <kanji>determine</kanji> that this is the best way to protect your coats from the elements.

There are <radical>hot peppers</radical> hiding in this <radical>dirt</radical>, so you build a <kanji>wall</kanji> to protect yourself from them. Have you ever stepped on a really hot pepper? The oils bleed into your shoes and burn your feet! You don't want anyone stepping on these so you wall them up.

The <radical>face</radical> radical and the <kanji>face</kanji> kanji are the same! But this isn't just a face like the one you're looking at your computer/phone with. It's the <kanji>surface</kanji> kind of face too!

Imagine contorting yourself in such a way that your <radical>mouth</radical> now has your <radical>legs</radical> inside it. You've bent your body in all the wrong ways to achieve this. In fact, if you look at yourself, you look like a square now... You're just a person with <kanji>four</kanji> sides.

The <radical>fins</radical> that fell onto my <radical>sword</radical> were sliced into many <kanji>part</kanji>s. Following this logic, this also means <kanji>minute</kanji>, which is part of an hour, and <kanji>divide</kanji>, which is the action of splitting something into many parts.
The <radical>giant</radical> radical and the <kanji>giant</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Massive!

The <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>book</radical> all about the human <kanji>body</kanji>. Knowing about bodies is a privilege. One that only leaders get—it helps them control all their subjects, like you.
Your <radical>leader</radical> is very <radical>anti</radical>-everything, making it all <kanji>temporary</kanji>. You get a new shirt, "I'm anti shirts!" she yells, and out it goes.

Play your <radical>drum</radical> under the <radical>moon</radical> and your <radical>hair</radical> will <kanji>swell</kanji>! The more you play and the longer it's under the moon, the larger your hair will swell.
What does your <radical>mama</radical> tell you to do in the <radical>street</radical>? Look before you cross! You gotta do it <kanji>beforehand</kanji>, or it could be too late.
When a <radical>canine</radical> puts its <radical>mouth</radical> on your <radical>heart</radical>, it's a pretty unforgettable <kanji>feeling</kanji>.
The <radical>compare</radical> radical and the <kanji>compare</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Huzzah!

Your new <radical>car</radical> doesn’t <radical>mix</radical> well with the other cars in your garage because its color is in stark <kanji>contrast</kanji> to the others. All of your other cars are pristine white in color, but this new car is a mix of lots of colors. It almost looks like a peacock or something it’s so colorful. Such a contrast.

Put your <radical>fingers</radical> to your <radical>forehead</radical> and stand your <radical>legs</radical> high up in a <radical>tree</radical> if you want to <kanji>look for</kanji> something. After all, shielding your eyes and getting up high is the best way to look for something, isn’t it?

You take an <radical>arrow</radical> to the <radical>mouth</radical> because you <kanji>know</kanji> too much. You know some very sensitive information, and someone's sent a bow-slinging enforcer to pin your big blabbermouth shut with arrows.

A <radical>leader</radical> with a <radical>spoon</radical> is a powerful thing. Even something as ordinary as a spoon can create great <kanji>change</kanji> in the world when given to the right person.

A <radical>cow</radical> with <radical>wing</radical>s is flying in the air. What a strange <kanji>thing</kanji>! You've heard of flying pigs before, but cows? This is a new and strange thing indeed! Cows with wings! What a silly thing.

<radical>Flowers</radical> are knocked down by a violent <radical>tsunami</radical>, and swirl through the air to <radical>kiss</radical> your face gently as they <kanji>fall</kanji>. With the tsunami came a great windy storm that blew the flowers down. Amongst all the destruction, it's a pretty sight to watch them fall.
There's the <radical>ground</radical>, and there's a <radical>toe</radical> just <kanji>below</kanji> it. Yep, you know this means "below" because the ground has a toe below it in this kanji.

When a <radical>thread</radical> <radical>loiter</radical>s on the <radical>horns</radical> of a <radical>coat rack</radical>, it's always <kanji>vertical</kanji>. By loitering I mean hanging around, and by horns I mean the little bits sticking off a coat rack that you hang your vertical threads on. Obviously.
The <radical>hole</radical> radical and the <kanji>hole</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Spectacular!

The <radical>leader</radical> put <radical>flowers</radical> on a <radical>cliff</radical>. That is their <radical>task</radical>. It is to <kanji>provide</kanji> and <kanji>equip</kanji> the cliff with flowers.
<radical>Flowers</radical> are <radical>mysterious</radical>ly appearing in the <radical>rice paddy</radical>. No matter what you do to them, they continue to <kanji>amass</kanji>.
You find a <radical>toe</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. It's weird, because it's <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, not where toes belong.

You see a <radical>thread</radical>-covered <radical>hill</radical>, but it's all lumpy and weird. You approach it to take a closer look, and you see it's actually a huge <kanji>net</kanji> with a bunch of stuff caught in it: fish, cats, tree branches. All this random stuff is what's making the net look so lumpy!

Lying on the <radical>ground</radical> is something that looks just like the ground, the number <kanji>One</kanji>. Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One doesn't have long to live.
In the <radical>cage</radical> there are some <radical>legs</radical>. They are the legs attached to a <kanji>small animal</kanji>.

In a <radical>private</radical> place on the <radical>moon</radical>, you hide two <radical>spoon</radical>s that give you a special <kanji>ability</kanji>. It's the ability to predict the future! The private moon spoons reflect the knowledge of time and space into your brain. Now that's a cool ability!
The radical and the meaning of this kanji are the same. Both are <kanji>power</kanji>.

A great <radical>leader</radical> can look <radical>straight</radical> at something and know its <kanji>value</kanji>. That social media site? 44 billion dollars. That job position? 50k a year. A four-day work week? Um… sorry, no, there's no value there. See? Leaders can see straight to the heart of matters to grasp something's true value.

When a <radical>drunkard</radical> holds a <radical>sword</radical> it <kanji>becomes</kanji> something else. The drunkard doesn't become a swordsman, they're still just a drunkard. But the sword in their hands does.

The <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>book</radical> all about the human <kanji>body</kanji>. Knowing about bodies is a privilege. One that only leaders get—it helps them control all their subjects, like you.
Cut this <radical>comb</radical> up with your <radical>weapon</radical> and turn it into the <kanji>steps</kanji> for some <kanji>stairs</kanji>.

Look at a <radical>building</radical> and <radical>compare</radical> the <radical>white</radical> parts. Each of those white parts is a <kanji>floor</kanji>. Multi-<kanji>story</kanji> buildings have tons of different floors that you can see from the outside.

When your <radical>self</radical> goes down a <radical>slide</radical> and hits a <radical>barb</radical>, it's a painful reminder that you have a <kanji>body</kanji>. Sometimes people think their self is just some kind of floating, disembodied consciousness, but getting a barb stuck in <kanji>oneself</kanji> while going down a slide will quickly remind one of one's bodily existence.

When something is <radical>heavy</radical>, you need a lot of <radical>power</radical> to <kanji>move</kanji> it. It’s not going to move unless you use a lot of power. That thing is heavy and unless you use all the power you got, it’s not moving.
The <radical>life</radical> radical and the <kanji>life</kanji> kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.

Imagining one’s <radical>self</radical> without a <radical>heart</radical> can make it hard to draw <kanji>breath</kanji>. Without a heart, you’d have no blood pumping and you would start to die. This would make it increasingly harder to draw breath and get oxygen to stay alive.
Killing two <radical>tree</radical>s with a <radical>jackhammer</radical> is expressly <kanji>prohibit</kanji>ed. Don't get me wrong, you can rip up one tree with your jackhammer, but not two.
If you only let <radical>oneself</radical> (yourself) into your <radical>heart</radical>, you'll end up <kanji>mourning</kanji> all of the loves and friendships you could have had but didn't let in.

You're known to get the ailment <radical>evening</radical> <radical>toe</radical>, which is when your toe catches on fire after getting really, really hot (imagine this happening, ouch!). The only way to fix it is to cool your toe down, and the best way to do that is to go <kanji>outside</kanji>.

Aim the <radical>scooter</radical>'s <radical>neck</radical> at the <kanji>road</kanji>! Scooters are only meant for driving on <kanji>street</kanji>s and <kanji>path</kanji>s, and if you go the wrong <kanji>way</kanji>, you'll probably break your own neck!

There's a <radical>gate</radical> that the <radical>sun</radical> goes through at a regular <kanji>interval</kanji>. There is an <kanji>interval of time</kanji> in the sense that the sun goes through it at the same time every day. And there is an <kanji>interval of space</kanji> in the sense that it has to go through the space between the gate posts.
The radical <radical>see</radical> and the kanji <kanji>see</kanji> are exactly the same!
The <radical>eye</radical> radical and <kanji>eye</kanji> kanji are the same as each other, so now you know them both.

What superhero is most like a <radical>triceratops</radical>? …If you said <radical>Wolverine</radical>, you're <kanji>correct</kanji>! Why is that the <kanji>right</kanji> answer? Because the triceratops has three horns, and Wolverine has three blades coming out of his hands. It's obvious, isn't it? But you knew that of course.

In a <radical>greenhouse</radical>, a <radical>towel</radical> becomes a <kanji>curtain</kanji> as soon as you hang it. Greenhouses are made of windows, it's all glass, there are no walls. So when you put a towel in there it turns into a curtain. It has no choice but to cover a window, after all.

There's a <radical>gate</radical> that the <radical>sun</radical> goes through at a regular <kanji>interval</kanji>. There is an <kanji>interval of time</kanji> in the sense that the sun goes through it at the same time every day. And there is an <kanji>interval of space</kanji> in the sense that it has to go through the space between the gate posts.

If you look really close at your <radical>rib cage</radical>, you'll see what looks like millions of tiny little <radical>thread</radical>s. But those aren't threads — they're the essential <kanji>element</kanji>s of life as we know it.

A <radical>tree</radical> with <radical>talent</radical> can become <kanji>lumber</kanji>. Not all trees can do this. Only the talented ones are able to turn themselves into lumber or <kanji>material</kanji>, to be used for a million different things.

In a dangerous <radical>fire</radical>, a <radical>gambler</radical> will always try to <kanji>bake</kanji> something before fleeing to safety. It could be his own house on fire, and the gambler would still use the free heat source to get some baking done. Maybe he's gambling that those baked goods will offset the cost of a new home…

This kanji also means <kanji>burn</kanji>, because that's what happens when you expose something to a lot of heat.
Your <radical>fingers</radical> are <radical>private</radical>, no one can see them unless they <kanji>pay</kanji>! And you decide if what they pay is enough to get a peek.
The <radical>compare</radical> radical and the <kanji>compare</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Huzzah!

Your new <radical>car</radical> doesn’t <radical>mix</radical> well with the other cars in your garage because its color is in stark <kanji>contrast</kanji> to the others. All of your other cars are pristine white in color, but this new car is a mix of lots of colors. It almost looks like a peacock or something it’s so colorful. Such a contrast.

A <radical>white</radical> guy jumps into a <radical>pool</radical> with a <kanji>target</kanji> painted on the bottom. He's aiming for the target, of course.

This kanji can also mean <kanji>-al</kanji>, as in the endings you get on some English adjectives. You know, like the pool is artifici-al. Or painting a target down there is not practic-al. That kind of -al.

If you feed a lot of <radical>beans</radical> to a <radical>geoduck</radical>, its <kanji>head</kanji> will grow larger. To a geoduck, beans are a brain food, so by eating beans, it can grow an incredibly large head. Geoducks already look strange enough as it is, let alone with engorged heads.
When your <radical>sick</radical> <radical>mama</radical> tries to do a <radical>task</radical> she's filled with <kanji>pain</kanji>!

Your <radical>ice</radical> <radical>wife</radical> is <kanji>amazing</kanji>. She's also <kanji>terrible</kanji>, because she's very cold and has many ice powers, but that's also part of what makes her amazing!

Lying on the <radical>ground</radical> is something that looks just like the ground, the number <kanji>One</kanji>. Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One doesn't have long to live.

There's a <radical>mouth</radical> inside a <radical>mouth</radical>. But if you look even closer (not actually, but we're pretending), you can see another mouth inside that mouth... and another one inside of that one! It keeps going and going. Can you count the number of <kanji>times</kanji> there's a mouth inside a mouth? This kanji also means <kanji>revolve</kanji> too. Think of each "time" a thing happens, it loops back around and comes back to the beginning, revolving around again another time.

If there is a <radical>building</radical> in the <radical>direction</radical> you want to go it will <kanji>prevent</kanji> you from going any further. Like, come on kid, you think you can just walk right through a building? Don't make me laugh!

They <radical>say</radical> that <radical>flowers</radical> held by a <radical>turkey</radical> on a <radical>stool</radical> are good to <kanji>defend</kanji> yourself with. Even better than a guard dog they say. People are so confused by the turkey sitting on a stool holding flowers that they just lose their will to fight. This turkey is great to defend yourself and your home.

Take the <radical>moon</radical> <radical>stamp</radical> on the <radical>stool</radical> in front of you and use it on all of your <kanji>clothes</kanji>. Now even if you sell or lose a piece of <kanji>clothing</kanji>, you'll be able to find it again by checking for your special moon stamp.
If you have a <radical>bow</radical> and a <radical>stick</radical> you need to <kanji>pull</kanji> the two back together to shoot it. If you can't pull it, you won't be shooting anything.
You find a <radical>toe</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. It's weird, because it's <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, not where toes belong.
<radical>Yakuza</radical> <radical>bars</radical> barely exist. The <kanji>remainder</kanji> of them aren't even in Japan.

You have a <radical>small</radical> <radical>slide</radical>. The thing about small slides is that <kanji>few</kanji> people can use them. Not everyone can fit their bum in a small slide — only a few small ones can make it down.

On a <radical>scooter</radical> there is a <radical>bear</radical> with a <radical>drop</radical> on its head trying to <kanji>follow</kanji> and <kanji>chase</kanji> you! You accidentally splashed it with water and it's not happy about it. You're on your own scooter, luckily, but that doesn't make this any less terrifying.

Touch your <radical>fingers</radical> to your <radical>friend</radical> to <kanji>pull out</kanji> their friendship. Pull it out with a high five. Pull it out with a back rub. Pull it out with an, "Oh no, there's some lint on your shirt, let me get that for you."

In a <radical>rice paddy</radical> you find a <radical>heart</radical> and it makes you <kanji>think</kanji>. You think a lot of <kanji>thought</kanji>s like, "Whose heart is this? Why is it in a rice paddy? Why am I thinking out loud?"
Under a <radical>cliff</radical> there's a <radical>stool</radical>. If someone has a stool deep inside a cliff, they are most likely pretty <kanji>anti</kanji>-society, hiding away like that.
You only have a <radical>few</radical> <radical>eye</radical>s so you need to <kanji>conserve</kanji> what's left.
There's an <radical>ear</radical> just sitting on a <radical>stool</radical>. Nobody's watching, so, you <kanji>take</kanji> it.

When you take your <radical>leader</radical>'s <radical>measurement</radical>s, you realize they're too short, so you <kanji>attach</kanji> things to them to make the measurements more impressive. Attach some heels to the shoes, then attach another head on top of theirs. There — perfect measurements thanks to these attachments.
You find a <radical>toe</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. It's weird, because it's <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, not where toes belong.

What was the biggest <radical>hole</radical> in the <radical>construction</radical> of the world? The <kanji>sky</kanji>. If you think about it, the sky is like a big hole someone forgot to fill in. That's why this kanji also means <kanji>empty</kanji>! Makes sense — nothing is more empty than the sky.
The <radical>moon</radical> grants a <radical>gladiator</radical> its <radical>power</radical> so he will <kanji>win</kanji>! <kanji>Victory</kanji> will be his!
The <radical>eye</radical> radical and <kanji>eye</kanji> kanji are the same as each other, so now you know them both.
The <radical>big</radical> radical and the <kanji>big</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
Your <radical>buddy</radical> the <radical>sheep</radical> is part of a <kanji>flock</kanji>. A flock of sheep. Your sheep buddy is a member of a flock.

There is a <radical>mountain</radical> on top of a bunch of <radical>dirt</radical> with a steep <radical>cliff</radical>. This is a bit redundant, but the mountain and the dirt just make the <kanji>cliff</kanji> even bigger and steeper and scarier.
Set up your <radical>canopy</radical>, <radical>attach</radical> some <radical>meat</radical> to it, and the meat will eventually <kanji>rot</kanji>.
If the <radical>yakuza</radical> hands you a <radical>spoon</radical>, <kanji>death</kanji> is coming for you. It's the death spoon, a sign that the yakuza is coming to cause your death.
A <radical>horse</radical> that is <radical>fat</radical> is a <kanji>burdensome</kanji> animal.
The radical <radical>heaven</radical> is the same as the kanji <kanji>heaven</kanji>, making this an easy kanji to memorize.

There is a <radical>lantern</radical> on top of a <radical>knife</radical> in the <radical>dirt</radical>. You walk up to take the knife and lantern when you realize it's just a <kanji>model</kanji>. It all looked real from far away, but now that you're up close you can see none of it is real.
The <radical>thread</radical> radical and <kanji>thread</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
